segments.s296-g169: This clause states existence with the archaic contracted copula: 'پسر پنج' here means 'five sons' (number after noun is poetic), followed by 'زنده‌ست' = 'is/are alive' where '-ست' is the older form of 'است'. 'پیشت' is the adverb 'near/at your side' formed from 'پیش' + enclitic 'ت' (your). The phrase 'به پای' literally 'at the foot (of)' is a poetic locative ('standing at your feet/side'). Word order is compact and somewhat inverted for metrical reasons.
segments.s296-g170: 'نمانیم' is first-person plural present (here jussive/intentive) with negative prefix 'ن-' meaning 'we do not/will not remain/stand by'. 'تا' is the subordinating conjunction 'until', which governs the following verb. 'تو کنی' uses the second-person singular present-subjunctive of کردن ('کنی') after 'تا' to express the action that must occur ('until you do/decide'). 'رزم رای' is a noun-noun collocation meaning 'decision/plan regarding battle' (رای = plan/decision), the object/goal of 'کنی'.
segments.s296-g171: The correlative negative construction 'نه ... نه' ('neither ... nor') links two subjects. 'لشکر پسندد' = 'the army approves' where پسندد is the archaic third-person singular form (‑د ending). 'ایزد پرست' is a compound meaning 'god-worshipper' or 'one who worships the deity' (here a noun); the second 'نه' negates it in parallel.
segments.s296-g172: 'که' here introduces the explanatory/causal clause ('for/because'). 'تو' is the second-person subject. 'جنگ ... را کنی' uses 'جنگ' (battle/war) as the object of کردن, marked by the object marker 'را' after the pronoun 'او' ('him') — literally 'you make war on him'. 'کنی' is again the second-person subjunctive/present form of کردن used in classical style; final adverb 'پیشدست' means 'in advance' or 'preemptively', explaining why neither army nor worshipper approves.
segments.s296-g33: چو is an archaic/poetic conjunction meaning 'when' here; it introduces a temporal subordinate clause. خسرو is the proper name (Khosrow) and is the subject. بدید is the simple past of دیدن 'to see' (he saw). آن سپاه is a deictic noun phrase 'that army' (آن = 'that', سپاه = 'army/host'). نیا is a noun placed after سپاه as a modifier or epithet (here 'of the ancestors' or 'ancestral'), a poetic/archaic word attached to describe the army.
segments.s296-g34: دل پادشا شد is a small clause where دل ('heart') is the subject and پادشا ('the king') apposes or clarifies whose heart it is; شد is the past of شدن 'became' (resultative). پر از is the adjective phrase 'full of' (پر + از), and کیمیا is the object of 'full of' — used metaphorically here as 'the elixir' or precious, transforming substance.
segments.s296-g35: This is a simple coordinate list introduced with خود ('he himself' or 'the king himself') followed by names joined with و ('and'). The sequence emphasizes that the king appears together with the famous heroes (Rostam, Tous, Goudarz, Giv).
segments.s296-g36: ز لشکر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/among the host' (ز = 'from/among', لشکر = 'army'). بسی is an intensifier meaning 'many' or 'much' in classical usage. نامبردار here means 'famous, renowned'. نیو is the noun 'ney' (reed‑flute) and is the item described as being numerous among the host; the copula is omitted as is common in poetry.
segments.s296-g37: همی + گشت forms a continuous or iterative past sense ('kept turning/was continually going'), a common poetic construction. بر گرد is a prepositional compound meaning 'around' (بر + گرد), and آن رزمگاه is the definite object 'that battlefield'. The overall clause means he repeatedly moved about the battlefield.
segments.s296-g38: This clause personifies the wilderness: بیابان ('the desert/wilderness') + نگه گرد (literally 'kept a watch/gaze around' — نگه here = 'watch/gaze' and گرد = 'around'). The final phrase بی‌راه و راه lists 'off‑path and path', i.e. in every direction or both off the road and on it; the conjunctions (و) simply coordinate the two contrasting terms.
segments.s296-g39: که introduces an explanatory/clausal link ('for/because/that'). لشکر فزون بود is a past-stative clause 'the army was numerous' (فزون = 'abundant/many', بود = 'was'). زان کو شمرد is a compact classical phrase: زان ('from/therefore/that'), کو here functions roughly as an archaic relative/indefinite ('who/that one who'), and شمرد is the past of شمردن 'to count' — together: 'so that whoever counted (it)...'.
segments.s296-g40: همان is an emphatic demonstrative 'that very/the same'. ژنده is an adjective/noun meaning 'rag(ged)' or 'tattered' qualifying پیلان (plural of پیل 'elephant') — 'ragged elephants'. و links to مردان 'men' (here 'crowds of men' or simply 'men'), and گرد at the end indicates 'around/about' or the gathering; the phrase lists what one actually saw when attempting to count the host.
segments.s296-g161: بدو گفت = 'he said to him'; بدو is the dative clitic-like prepositional form 'to him/unto him' (older/poetic order puts it before the verb), and گفت is the past tense 'said' (here used as a simple reporting verb). کای کار نادیده مرد is a vocative phrase: کای = 'O!' addressing someone; کار نادیده = 'an unwise/heedless deed' (نادر دیده/نادیده = not seen, heedless), and مرد = 'man' — together 'O man of heedless deeds'.
segments.s296-g162: شهنشاه کی جوید از تو نبرد is a question. شهنشاه = 'the shahanshah/sovereign'. کی here is the interrogative 'who/which' (not 'when'). جوید is present/subjunctive/optative sense 'would seek' or simple 'seeks' (classical Persian allows this form for potential). از تو نبرد = 'a battle from you' i.e. 'a battle with you' (از + pronoun marks the other side/party).
segments.s296-g163: اگر جویدی هم نبردش منم is conditional: اگر = 'if'. جویدی is 2nd person singular present/optative 'you seek' (here 'if you were to seek/if you seek'); هم = 'also/even' emphasizing the speaker's agreement; نبردش = 'his battle' (نبرد + enclitic ـش = 'his'); منم = 'I too' or 'I am (also)' — an elliptical clause meaning 'I too will be [involved/available]'.
segments.s296-g164: تن و نام او زیر پای افگنم = 'I will cast his body and name under (my) foot'. تن = 'body/person'; و = 'and'; نام = 'name' here meaning 'reputation/fame' (not merely label); او = 'his/he'; زیر پای = 'beneath the foot/underfoot'; افگنم is an archaic first‑person verb form (from افگن/افکندن) meaning 'I will cast/throw down' — a committed, somewhat poetic future/action of humiliating/overthrowing.
segments.s296-g165: گر او با من آید به آوردگاه = a conditional clause: گر (classical 'if'); او = 'he'; با من = 'with me'; آید = 3rd person singular present/subjunctive 'comes' (classical form used in conditionals); به آوردگاه = 'to the field/battlefield' (به + place noun). Word order is subject + adjunct + verb + destination.
segments.s296-g166: برآساید از جنگ هر دو سپاه = 'will find ease/ be relieved from war, the two armies'. برآساید is a 3rd person singular subjunctive/future-like verb 'will be eased/relieved' (برآسیدن = to be appeased/relieved). از جنگ = 'from war' and هر دو سپاه = 'both armies' (literally 'each of the two hosts') — together meaning the two armies' fighting will cease/become relieved.
segments.s296-g167: بدو شیده گفت ای جهاندیده مرد = 'to him the shideh/lover said: O worldly‑wise man'. بدو = 'to him'; شیده is an epithet 'shideh' = 'love‑struck' or 'mad (with love)' (used as a substantive 'the lover' or adjective describing the speaker); گفت = 'said' (reporting verb). ای جهاندیده مرد is a vocative: ای = 'O', جهاندیده = 'worldly‑wise/experienced in the world', مرد = 'man'.
segments.s296-g168: چشیده ز گیتی بسی گرم و سرد = 'you have tasted/experienced of the world much heat and cold'. چشیده (perfect participle) = 'having tasted/experienced' (used adjectivally of the addressee); ز = poetic form of 'از' = 'from/of'; گیتی = 'the world'; بسی = 'much/very' (classical intensifier); گرم و سرد = 'hot and cold' (metaphor for ups and downs). The participial construction sums up: 'you have experienced many vicissitudes of the world.'
segments.s296-g9: This clause is a short verb phrase in past tense: subject is omitted and supplied by the following noun phrase. سپاهی (a troop/army) is the direct object, ز + جنگ آوران is a prepositional phrase 'from the war-bringers/warriors' (ز = 'from' in poetic register), and برگزید is the past tense verb 'chose/selected'. Word order is object + adjunct + verb, common in older Persian poetry.
segments.s296-g10: This is the agent phrase and a final adverbial: بزرگان ایران is the nominative agent 'the great ones/elders of Iran', followed by چنانچون سزید, where چنانچون ('as, just as') introduces a simile/qualification and سزید (from سزا/سزایدن) here means 'was fitting / befitted' (an intransitive perfective). The whole phrase qualifies the action as done 'as befitted' the grandees.
segments.s296-g11: چشیده is a perfect/past participle meaning 'has tasted/has experienced' and governs the rest of this noun phrase. بسی here intensifies (classical 'much/very'), followed by از جهان 'of the world/this life' and the coordinated nouns شور و تلخ 'tumult/passion and bitter' describing what was experienced. Word order places the experiencer verb first, then the quantified source phrase.
segments.s296-g12: This clause expresses direction/purpose. به یاری 'for the help/of help' indicates purpose, گستهم is an archaic 1st-person past-form 'I wandered / I went about' (used by the poetic narrator), and نوذر به بلخ places the beneficiary/place: 'to/for Nowzar at Balkh' (به before بلخ marks the destination). In modern prose you'd render it 'went to Balkh to help Nowzar.'
segments.s296-g13: به اشکش functions as an instrumental phrase 'with/by his tear' (به marking means). بفرمود is the honorific past 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'), and سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'. زم is an archaic locative/oblique noun (here 'the land / the place'), used with سوی to give the destination. The clause as a whole: 'by his tears he ordered so that (they) would carry toward the land...'.
segments.s296-g14: This is the content of the ordered action: برد is here a verb meaning 'carry/take' (classical usage), followed by a series of coordinated direct objects: لشکر 'army', پیل 'elephants', and گنج درم 'treasure (in dirhams)'. The coordination uses repeated و in the poetic style; درم specifies the coin/treasure form.
segments.s296-g15: بدان here is built from the prepositional/imperative components ب + دان and functions as a conjunctive/performative 'do this / know this' introducing purpose; تا marks purpose 'so that', پس 'after/thereafter' is temporal, اندر 'in/within' is a locative preposition, نیاید is a negated present/subjunctive verb 'may not come / shall not come' (classical negative form), and سپاه is the noun 'army'. Together: 'Do this so that thereafter the army not come within.'
segments.s296-g16: کند is the present/subjunctive 3rd-person form of کردن 'to do/make' used here to form a result clause ('and thus makes/causes'), رای means 'plan/counsel' (the object), شیران is a classical epithet 'lions' used metaphorically for brave men/warriors, ایران here refers to the people/realm, and تباه is an adjective 'ruined/destroyed'. The clause states the consequence: 'and thus the counsel of Iran's heroes is ruined.'
segments.s296-g89: ز (short for از) is a poetic preposition 'of/from' placed before خوبی ('goodness, kindness'). نگه کن is an imperative compound ('keep/guard/heed') made of نگه (watch/keeping) + کن (imperative 'do'). که introduces the following content clause ('for/that'), پیران is the genitive possessor 'the elders', چه کرد is an interrogative/exclamatory-style clause meaning 'what they did' (چه = 'what', کرد = past 'did'). The whole segment = 'Beware/guard against goodness, for see what the elders did.'
segments.s296-g90: بدان here is an older/poetic form where ب- functions as a prefix and دان relates to 'know/consider' (imperative/indicative sense 'know/consider that' or a demonstrative 'that'); it points to the person being spoken of. بی‌وفا and ناسزاوار are stacked adjectives modifying مرد ('man/person'). Together the phrase means 'that faithless, reproachable man' (literally 'know/that unfaithful, reproach‑worthy man').
segments.s296-g91: همه 'all/everything' modifies مهر پیران 'the elders' affection/kindness' (مهر = love/affection, + پیران = 'of the elders'). فراموش کرد is the past-tense compound 'forgot' (فراموش = forget, کرد = did → made/was). Word order is possessive noun phrase then verb: 'He forgot all the elders' affection.'
segments.s296-g92: پر از ... کرد is a causative/result phrase 'made (something) full of ...' where پر از = 'full of' and کرد is the past verb applying to the whole coordinated phrase. The line uses two parallel nominal phrases: 'سر دل پر از کینه' = 'the head/brain and heart full of hatred' and 'دل پر از جوش' = 'the heart full of boiling/ardor' (جوش = boiling/ardor). In short, 'he filled (his) head with hatred and (his) heart with boiling passion/anger.'
segments.s296-g93: همی بود خامش is a literary past construction meaning 'he was/kept silent' (همی often gives an iterative or continuous shade; بود = was; خامش = quiet/silent). چو آمد = 'when he came' (چو = when), and به مشت literally 'to/into the fist' is an idiom meaning 'when he came to striking/blows' (مشت = fist/strike). So: 'He was silent until he came to striking.'
segments.s296-g94: چنان 'thus/so' modifies the following clause. مهربان پهلوان is the noun phrase 'the kind/merciful champion/hero' and را is the direct‑object marker. بکشت is an archaic/past verbal form (classical prefix بـ on the root کشتن) meaning 'he killed.' The whole segment: 'Thus he killed the kind hero.'
segments.s296-g95: از ایران کنون با سپاهی به جنگ is a straightforward clause: از ایران 'from Iran', کنون 'now', با سپاهی 'with an army/with soldiers' and به جنگ 'to battle/for war' (به + جنگ as direction/purpose). Word order is temporal/adverbial elements followed by agent and purpose.
segments.s296-g96: بیامد is the past 'came/appeared'. به پیش = 'into the presence of/before' (پیش = in front/in the presence of). نیا تیزچنگ is a proper name here (Niyâ Tizchang); in classical Persian names often appear as two-word compounds. Thus: 'he came before Niyâ Tizchang.'
segments.s296-g65: شود is a 3rd-person singular verb here meaning “become” (an optative/conditional sense common in classical Persian). The word order is poetically inverted: subject 'کوه آهن' (an iron mountain) is followed by the simile marker 'چو' and the simile phrase 'دریای آب' (a sea of water) while the verb 'شود' appears at the beginning for emphasis.
segments.s296-g66: 'اگر' introduces the conditional clause “if.” 'بشنود' is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive of شنیدن used here in the sense “he/it hears” (used for potential events). 'نام افرسیاب' means “the name/reputation of Afrasiab” — the object follows the verb in this classical order.
segments.s296-g67: The clause begins with the subject 'زمین' (the earth). 'بر' is a spatial/parenthetical preposition here ('upon/against'), and 'نتابد' (negated form of تابیدن) functions as “does not endure/does not bear” — a present/gnomic negation. 'سپاه' takes the 2nd-person possessive suffix 'ت' (your) and the object/topic marker 'را', so 'سپاه ترا' = 'your army/host.'
segments.s296-g68: 'نه' = 'nor'; the subject phrase 'خورشید تابان' = 'the shining/radiant sun'. The verb from the previous clause ('نتابد') is omitted here by ellipsis and understood to apply again. 'کلاه ترا' (کلاه + 2nd-person suffix + را) means 'your crown/hat' — object with possessive suffix.
segments.s296-g69: نیاید is a negative present/optative meaning 'does not come / will not come.' 'ز شاهان' = 'of/from the kings' (poetic genitive), and 'کسی' in the scope of the negation functions as 'no one' here. 'پیش تو' = 'before you / in your presence.'
segments.s296-g70: This is an exception clause introduced by 'جز' ('except'). 'این بی‌پدر' = 'this fatherless one'; 'بد گوهر' is a compound epithet meaning literally 'bad/evil gem' (i.e. a worthless or ill‑favored person). 'خوی' + the enclitic 'ش' form خویش (one's own), and the final 'تو' clarifies possession: 'your own (bad) gem.' Word order is compressed and possessive morphology is split across words.
segments.s296-g71: 'سیاووش را' marks Siavash as the definite object with 'را.' 'چون' = 'as' (meaning 'in the capacity of'), and 'پسر داشتی' uses داشتی (past second-person) to mean 'you had/kept (as) a son' — a straightforward past-tense possession/relationship expression.
segments.s296-g72: 'بر او' = 'toward him / upon him.' The coordinated nouns 'رنج و مهر' mean 'suffering (pain) and affection (love)'; 'پدر' qualifies them as 'a father's' (paternal) — together 'رنج و مهر پدر' = 'a father's pain and love.' 'داشتی' repeats the past-tense verb 'you had/you felt.'
segments.s296-g25: This is a locative phrase: به (to/on) + چپ (left) + بر (on/upon) + دهستان (place name). Persian often omits the copular verb 'بود' (was), so the clause means “on the left was Dehestān.” The second half repeats the locative pattern (و 'and' + بر راست آب) to say “and on the right (was) water.”
segments.s296-g26: میان (between) starts a short prepositional phrase linking two things; ریگ is the noun 'sand'. The sequence 'و پیش اندر افراسیاب' uses پیش 'before/in front of' plus اندر 'in/within' to locate Afrasiāb ahead of them. Word order puts the place (Afrasiāb) after the locative prepositions.
segments.s296-g27: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. سر زد is a poetic/periphrastic past (literally 'struck its head') used idiomatically to mean 'rose/appeared'. ز برج is the prepositional phrase 'from (the) tower'. بره is an epithet/poetic word here (literally 'lamb') used as a descriptive flourish following برج.
segments.s296-g28: بیاراست is a past-tense verb 'adorned' (bi- + آراستن). روی زمین is the direct object phrase 'the face/surface of the earth' (روی here = surface/face). یکسره is an adverb 'entirely/completely' modifying the verb phrase and placed after the object for emphasis.
segments.s296-g29: سپهدار ترکان is a genitive construction 'the commander of the Turks' (title + group). سپه را uses the object marker را to mark 'the army' as definite object, and بدید is the simple past 'saw'. Word order is Subject (سپهدار ترکان) + Object (سپه را) + Verb (بدید).
segments.s296-g30: This gives two coordinated actions: بزد نای رویین is Verb + Object (he struck the نایِ رویین, literally 'iron/metal flute'—here functioning like a trumpet). The conjunction و links the second clause صف برکشید, where برکشید (past tense) = 'lifted/raised/drew up' and صف = 'the battle line/ranks', so 'he formed/drew up the ranks.'
segments.s296-g31: جهان شد is a copular construction 'the world became', and پر here is the adjective 'full (of)'; together 'the world became full of...'. آوای بوق is a genitive/construct 'the sound (آواز) of trumpets', so the clause reads 'the world became full of the sound of trumpets.' The coordinating و then introduces the next clause.
segments.s296-g32: همه (all/everyone) is the subject; برنهادند is the past plural 'they raised/took up' (bar-nahādan). ز آهن is an ablative/genitive-like phrase 'of/from iron' qualifying کلاه (helmet), so the phrase means 'they all raised (or took up) their iron helmets.' Word order is Subject + Verb + Qualifier + Object.
segments.s296-g129: This clause opens with the causal/prepositional phrase 'از آن کو' (literary/archaic), meaning 'because of / on account of that one who...'. 'کو' here is an archaic relative ('who/that') rather than a locative; 'بر این دشت' is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon this plain' (preposition + demonstrative + noun). 'کین کُشته شد' is a passive construction: 'کشته شد' uses شد as the auxiliary (past passive), so 'was killed' (kشته = 'killed', شد = auxiliary). Word order is literary: [prepositional/circumstantial phrase] + [location] + [cause/reason] + [passive verb].
segments.s296-g130: 'زمین زیر او' = 'the ground beneath him' (noun + preposition + pronoun). 'چون' introduces a simile meaning 'like/as'. 'گل آغشته شد' is a stative/result clause: 'آغشته شد' uses شد in the sense 'became' (became soaked/imbued), so 'was/grew soaked (with ...)'; 'گل' here is used in the sense 'mud/clay' (poetic image of the earth stained). The overall sequence is subject ('زمین...') + simile phrase + result verb.
segments.s296-g131: 'همه مرز توران' = 'all the borders/frontiers of Turan' (quantifier + noun + proper name). The predicate 'شکسته دلند' is a poetic, slightly inverted expression meaning 'their hearts are broken' or 'they are broken‑hearted': 'شکسته' is an adjective/participial modifier and 'دلند' functions as a predicate (poetic plural formation meaning 'they are hearts / their heart is'). The copular sense ('are') is implicit in 'ند' ending.
segments.s296-g132: 'ز تیمار دل را' gives cause: 'ز' (poetic variant of از) here means 'from / because of'; 'تیمار دل را' = 'the care/solicitude of the heart' where 'دل را' is the definite/direct‑object phrase (را marks the object). 'همی' is an archaic/poetic present‑continuous/adverb meaning 'continually / always'. 'بگسلند' is present (the b‑prefix marks imperfective) third‑person plural 'they break / rend' (here sense 'to break/rend'). Word order places the causal prepositional phrase before the verb, standard in poetry.
segments.s296-g133: 'نبینند' is negative present (literary) third‑person plural 'they do not see' (here: 'they see nothing'); 'جز' = 'except / other than'; 'مرگ پیران' = 'the death of the elders' (noun + genitive); 'به خواب' = 'in sleep / in dreams' (preposition + noun). The clause means 'in their sleep they see nothing but the elders' death' — negative verb + exception phrase + temporal/locative complement.
segments.s296-g134: This short clause uses 'نخواند' (negative) meaning 'will not / does not call/sing/utter' (here 'utter' or 'mention'); word order is verb + subject 'نخواند کسی' giving the sense 'no one will utter'. 'نام افراسیاب' is the object ('the name Afrasiab'). In older/literary Persian the verb often precedes the subject for emphasis.
segments.s296-g135: 'بباشیم' is a jussive/subjunctive form meaning 'let us be' or 'we should be' (imperative/jussive sense). 'تا' here means 'so that / in order that' introducing the purpose/result clause. 'نامداران ما' = 'our illustrious/renowned men' (noun + possessive). The phrase sets up a wish/intention: 'let it be that our famed men...'
segments.s296-g136: This continues the purpose clause: 'مهان' = 'guests' (predicate / role assigned to 'نامداران ما'), 'و' = 'and' links the next idea, 'ز لشکر سواران ما' = 'from the host (army), our horsemen' (z = 'from/of', لشکر = 'host/army', سواران ما = 'our horsemen'). The clause is elliptical (typical of epic diction) and should be read as continuing the wish begun in the previous segment.
segments.s296-g145: مبارز پراگنده is a noun phrase (mubāraz + adjective) meaning ‘scattered fighters’. بیرون is an adverb/particle meaning ‘out/away’ placed before the verb; کنیم is the 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/hortative of کردن used here in a hortatory/future sense (‘let us/ we will cause to go out’). The verb therefore takes the preceding phrase as its object: ‘we will drive out the scattered fighters.’
segments.s296-g146: از ایشان is a prepositional phrase ‘from them’. بیابان پر از خون is the noun phrase ‘the plain/ wasteland full of blood’ where پر از uses از as the complement marker (‘full of’). کنیم repeats the same 1st-person-plural form ‘we will / let us make’, so the clause reads ‘from them we will make the plain full of blood’ (i.e. cover the plain with their blood).
segments.s296-g147: چنین داد پاسخ is an inverted poetic order: چنین ‘thus’ + داد ‘he gave’ + پاسخ ‘an answer’ — literally, ‘thus he gave answer.’ که‌ای should be read as که + ای (که introducing the speech/clause, ای as the vocative particle), so here it introduces direct address: ‘that, O king…’. شهریار is the vocative ‘king.’
segments.s296-g148: چو functions here as a conditional ‘if’ (classical usage). ز is the poetic genitive/partitive ‘from/of’ and این‌گونه ‘of this kind/ such’ modifies the noun. جویی is the classical 2nd-person-present form of جستن/جوییدن ‘you seek’ (here treated as ‘you seek’), and همی is the archaic progressive/continuous particle marking ongoing action. کارزار is the object ‘battle/contest’. Together: ‘If you seek such a battle…’
segments.s296-g149: نخستین ‘first/foremost’ followed by ز لشکر مبارز ‘of the fighting host’ (ز = ‘of’ in poetic usage). منم is the emphatic copula ‘I am’ (من + -م enclitic), so the clause states identity/position: ‘I am the foremost of the fighting host.’
segments.s296-g150: که is the relative pronoun ‘who/that’ introducing a relative clause. بر شیر و بر پیل are two prepositional phrases ‘upon the lion and upon the elephant’. اسب is the object ‘horse/steed’. افگنم is an archaic/poetic 1st-person singular form of افگندن (here used in a causative/sudden-action sense ‘I fling/ I cast’ or ‘I set (my steed) upon’), so the clause means ‘who fling (my) steed upon lion and elephant.’ Word order places the prepositional phrases before the object and verb for poetic effect.
segments.s296-g151: کسی را ندانم is a classical negative construction meaning ‘I know no one’ (کسی ‘anyone’ with the object marker را; ندانم = ‘I do not know’). که introduces the relative clause ‘who…’. روز نبرد literally ‘the day of battle’ (روز = day, نبرد = battle), setting the temporal circumstance for the relative clause.
segments.s296-g152: فشاند is a 3rd-person form of افشاندن/فشاندن ‘to scatter, to fling/throw’ linked back to the relative (کسی)… ‘who throws’. بر اسب من = ‘at/on my horse’ (بر = on/against, اسب من = my horse). از دور = ‘from a distance/afar’. گرد here functions adverbially as ‘around/about’ (classical phrasing) — together: ‘who throws from afar at my horse.’
segments.s296-g17: از آن پس is an adverbial phrase meaning “from that time on/thereafter” placed before the verb. یلان را همه is the definite/ topicalized object: یلان (warriors/heroes) marked by را and followed by همه for emphasis = “all the warriors.” برنشاند is a literary/past narrative verb (بر- + نشاند), meaning “placed/stationed/arrayed” and takes یلان را همه as its object.
segments.s296-g18: بزد is a past narrative verb “he struck” and takes کوس رویین as its object: کوس (war‑drum) modified by رویین “iron/brazen.” The conjunction و links this clause to the next; لشکر براند is a coordinated clause where لشکر (the host/army) is the object/topic and براند (بر- + راند) is a past/actional verb meaning “drove/set in motion” or “led on.”
segments.s296-g19: همی here is a classical/epic adverb marking continuous or habitual action (“kept on/continued to”). رفت is the past verb “he went/proceeded.” با introduces an adverbial list (رای و هوش و درنگ) of accompanying qualities: رای = “counsel/plan,” هوش = “intellect/awareness,” درنگ = “delay/deliberation” (a deliberate pause).
segments.s296-g20: که introduces a subordinate causal/explanatory clause here (“for/because”). تیزی is used as “swiftness/speed,” پشیمانی is the object “regret/remorse,” and آرد is the classical 3rd‑person present/past narrative of آوردن meaning “brings.” به جنگ is a prepositional phrase meaning “in/into battle,” completing the clause “for swiftness brings remorse in battle.”
segments.s296-g21: سپهدار is the subject “the commander.” چون functions as a temporal conjunction here (“when/as”), followed by the locative phrase در بیابان “in the plain/wilderness.” رسید is the past verb “arrived/reached,” so the whole clause reads “when the commander reached the plain.”
segments.s296-g22: This is a list of what he beheld: گرازیدن here names a charge/onrush (literally “to charge like a boar”), ساز refers to the musical instruments or martial music, and لشکر is the army/host; بدید is the past verb “he saw/beheld.” The coordination places these as the sights that greeted him on arrival.
segments.s296-g23: سپه را uses را to topicalize/mark ‘the army’ (“as for the army”), followed by گذر “passage/route.” سوی خورازم is the directional phrase “toward Khwarazm,” and بود is the copula in the past sense “was.” Together: “As for the army, its passage was toward Khwarazm.”
segments.s296-g24: همه modifies the coordinated nouns رنگ و دشت (“all the color/appearance and the plain”), an idiomatic way to say the entire field/landscape; از در رزم بود is a poetic locative/causal phrase meaning literally “was from/in the state of battle” — i.e. “was filled with/marked by battle.” The clause therefore says the whole plain showed the signs/appearance of war.
segments.s296-g41: به گرد سپه is a locative prepositional phrase meaning “around the army” (به + گرد = around). بر یکی places the action “on one (side)” (بر = on/upon, یکی = one). کنده کرد is a compound verbal phrase: کنده (past participle ‘dug/uprooted’) plus the light verb کرد, giving a completed action “dug” or “caused to be dug.” Word order is typical poetic Persian (locative phrase before the verb).
segments.s296-g42: طلایه is the subject/noun ‘the vanguard’ or ‘forerunners.’ به هر سو is a directional phrase ‘to every side’ (به = toward/into, هر سو = every direction). پراگنده is an adjective/participial form ‘scattered,’ and کرد is the light verb ‘made/did,’ so پراگنده کرد = ‘(he) scattered (them).’
segments.s296-g43: شب آمد is a simple verbal clause ‘night came/fell.’ به کنده در is a locative phrase ‘into the dug hole/ditch’ where کنده is a past participle ‘dug’ modifying در (here ‘hole/ditch’), and افگند is the past tense verb ‘threw/ poured’ with آب (water) as its object following it. The sequence shows the participial modifier before the noun and a final verb with its object — typical classical order.
segments.s296-g44: بدان سو is an archaic locative ‘to that side’ (بدان = to that — here written as ב + دان in the manuscript tokens — and سو = side/direction). که introduces a relative clause ‘which/that,’ بد روی is an adjectival phrase ‘facing (badly/hostile/ opposite)’ where بد qualifies روی (face/direction), and افراسیاب is the proper name Afrasiab. Together: ‘to that side which faced Afrasiab.’
segments.s296-g45: دو لشکر is the noun phrase ‘two armies.’ چنین هم is an adverbial phrase ‘thus/so as well’ (چنین = thus; هم = also/indeed), and دو روز و دو شب simply gives the duration ‘two days and two nights.’ The main verb is implied from context (they remained/continued so).
segments.s296-g46: از ایشان = ‘from them’ (از = from, ایشان = they/them). یکی را uses کسی/یکی with the object marker را to mean ‘not one (of them)’; نجنبید is the negative past ‘did not move/stir’ (3rd pl. negation), and لب ‘lip’ is used idiomatically: ‘not a lip moved’ = they were completely still/silent. Word order has the object (یکی را) before the verb, and لب is a short noun adjunct emphasizing silence.
segments.s296-g47: تو گفتی is the past tense ‘you said.’ که introduces the content clause (‘that’). روی زمین is a genitive/compound ‘the surface of the earth’ (روی = surface/face, زمین = earth), and آهن است is the copula clause ‘is iron.’ Note گفتی is a past verb (speaker addressing ‘you’), and the clause that follows is what was said.
segments.s296-g48: ز نیزه = ‘from/since (there are) spears’ (ز = from/because of, نیزه = spear). هوا here means ‘the air/sky’ (i.e. the space above), and نیز ‘also/likewise’ links the clause. در جوشن = ‘in/among the armor’ (در = in, جوشن = armor). است is the copula. Taken together the line says that spears fill the air and armor is present there as well — a poetic way of saying weapons and armor are everywhere.
segments.s296-g113: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a past tense 3rd-person form 'he heard' placed before the subject (stylistic/poetic V–S order). افراسیاب is the subject (proper name) and این سخن is the direct object 'these/the foregoing words'. Together: 'When Afrasiab heard these words...'.
segments.s296-g114: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', a prepositional/indirect object phrase). مشتاب and تندی مکن are two imperatives: مشتاب = 'hurry!' (imperative), و links the commands, and تندی مکن = negative imperative 'do not be hasty/violent'. The clause is direct speech reported after 'he said to him'.
segments.s296-g115: سخن هرچه گفتی = 'the speech/whatsoever you said' — هرچه is a free relative 'whatever/whatever it was that', and گفتی here is the 2nd-person past 'you said' (poetic word order places سخن first). همه راست بود = 'was all true' (همه intensifies completeness; بود is past 'was').
segments.s296-g116: جز از راستی را = 'except (that which is) of truth' with را serving as the definite/object marker/emphatic particle; نباید + شنود is an impersonal/modal construction 'should not be heard' or 'ought not to be listened to' (نباید = 'must/should not', شنود is an older/poetic verb form 'hear'). The clause restricts acceptable speech to truth alone.
segments.s296-g117: ولیکن = 'but/yet' introducing contrast. تو دانی = present 'you know' (دانی is 2nd-person present of دانستن). که introduces a subordinate content clause. پیران گرد is a terse classical phrase: پیران = 'the elders', گرد here carries the sense 'around/assembled/about' (poetic adjective/phrase marking the elders as a group).
segments.s296-g118: به گیتی = 'to/for the world' (به + noun). همه راه نیکی = 'every/all the paths of goodness' (همه quantifies راه نیکی). سپرد is past 'entrusted/handed over/committed' (3rd-person past; in classical style it can agree with a plural subject without -ند). The clause says the elders committed every path of goodness to the world.
segments.s296-g119: نبد is an archaic negative/existential form meaning 'there was not' (classical variant of نبود). در دلش = 'in his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = 'his'). کژی و کاستی = 'crookedness and lack/deficiency' (two nouns joined by و). The clause states the absence of vice or shortcoming in his heart.
segments.s296-g120: نجستی is the 2nd-person past form of جستن used here in the sense 'you find / you would discover' (classical narrative addressing the addressee: 'you would not find ... except ...'). به جز = 'except', followed by the exceptions خوبی و راستی = 'goodness and truth'. Together: 'you would find nothing except goodness and truth.'
segments.s296-g105: بدیشان is the prepositional phrase به + ایشان 'to/for them' placed before the rest; چه باید is an interrogative + necessity verb (باید 'must/ought'), here asking 'what need / what must (be done)'. ستاره شمر is the noun phrase 'the star of Shamr' (ستاره used metaphorically) placed after the question phrase as its topic/object; شمر is a proper name. Word order is poetic (topic before verb).
segments.s296-g106: به here marks instrument or means ('by/with'), شمشیر is the instrument 'sword', جویند is 3rd-person plural present (they seek/pursue) with the verb before its subject (poetic order), and مردان هنر is the plural subject 'men/heroes of skill' (هنر = skill). The clause therefore says that men of skill pursue/seek by the sword.
segments.s296-g107: سواران is the head noun 'horsemen/riders'; که introduces a relative clause 'who'. در میمنه is a locative phrase 'on the right (flank)' and با منند means 'are with me' (an older/poetic form: با + من + ـند), indicating these riders accompany the speaker on his right. Word order places the relative clause immediately after the noun it modifies.
segments.s296-g108: همه is the universal 'all/everyone'; جنگ را is the object/topic marked with را ('the whole fight/battle'); یکدل is an adjective 'one‑hearted' (united in purpose) and یکتنند is a verblike predicate 'they are one/identical' (poetic plural predicate). Together the predicate describes complete unity in battle.
segments.s296-g109: چو here is conditional 'if/when'; دستور باشد uses باشد (subjunctive/present of 'to be') to mean 'an order be' or 'there is an order/command'; مرا is the first‑person object 'to me' (poetic word order places it before or inside the clause); and پادشا is 'the king' (addressed as agent of the order). The whole clause means 'if the king orders (it) of me'.
segments.s296-g110: از ایشان is a partitive/prepositional phrase 'from them'; نمانم is 1st‑person singular negative ('I will not remain / I will not be left') used to mean 'I will not leave'; یکی پارسا is the noun phrase 'one (even one) pious/virtuous (person)'. The clause thus says the speaker will leave not one pious person among them.
segments.s296-g111: بدوزم is 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of دوختن 'to sew/fasten' used here as a violent image 'I will fasten/impale'. سر و ترگ ایشان is the compound object 'their heads and rags/shreds', and به تیر indicates the target/instrument 'to/onto the arrows'. The verb before the object and the prepositional phrase after reflect poetic order.
segments.s296-g112: نه negates the verb; اندیشم is 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of اندیشیدن 'I think/care' meaning 'I will not consider/be concerned'. از introduces the matters not considered ('about/regarding'), and کنده و آبگیر are two nouns 'stump/uprooted trunk' and 'pond/reservoir' respectively. Together: 'I will not bother (about) stump or pool.'
segments.s296-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. آگاه شد is a compound in which آگاه (aware) is predicative and شد (past of شدن) marks a change of state 'became aware'. شهریار جهان is the subject phrase 'the sovereign/king of the world' (noun + epithet), placed after the predicate in the classical poetic word order.
segments.s296-g2: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/through/because of' introducing the cause or source. گفتار بیدار کارآگهان is a noun phrase: گفتار 'words/speech' qualified by بیدار 'vigilant/alert' and کارآگهان (plural of کارآگه) 'the informed/informing men' — together 'the warning words of the watchful informants'. The whole phrase gives the means/ground for آگاه شد.
segments.s296-g3: This is a pair of ablative phrases joined: ز ترکان 'from/because of the Turks' and وز کار افراسیاب where وز should be read as the archaic concatenation 'و ز' = 'and from' (i.e. 'and from/and because of') linking the second phrase 'کار افراسیاب' 'the deed/action of Afrasiab'. In other words: 'from the Turks and from Afrasiab's action'.
segments.s296-g4: که is the relative/connecting 'who/which/that' referring back to Afrasiab; لشکرگه آورد is the verb phrase where لشکرگه 'army-camp/encampment' is the object and آورد (past of آوردن) 'brought/moved' is the verb. ز این روی آب is a prepositional phrase: ز = 'from/of' (classical), این روی آب 'this side/surface of the water' (بدelivery: 'to/from this side of the river') giving the origin/direction of the camp. Together: 'who brought his camp from/across the water to this side.'
segments.s296-g5: سپاهی is the subject 'an army/host'. ز جیحون is the ablative 'from the Jeyhun' (Jeyhun = Amu Darya). بدین سو is the classical directional expression composed of ب + این + سو meaning 'to this side/toward here'. کشید is the past verb (from کشیدن) here used to mean 'moved/drew/crossed' — together: 'an army moved from the Jeyhun to this side.'
segments.s296-g6: که introduces a result or consequence clause 'so that/and then'. شد here is 'became/it came to pass that' and governs the nominal phrase ریگ و سنگ 'sand and stone' as the subjects that follow. از جهان 'from the world' gives the domain, and ناپدید is the predicate adjective 'vanished/gone' — together: 'so that sand and stone were gone from the world.'
segments.s296-g7: چو is 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (classical word order often places the verb before the later explicit subject); خسرو is the named subject 'Khosrow'. یلان را (with the object marker را) is the object 'the heroes' and بخواند is past 'he called/summoned' (here 'to call/summon'). The clause means 'When Khosrow heard (this), he summoned the heroes.'
segments.s296-g8: همه گفتنی is a nominal group meaning 'all those who had something to say' (literally 'all sayable/things to be said' used as 'everyone with words to say'). پیش ایشان 'in their presence / before them' is the prepositional phrase. براند (from راندن) is a third-person verb here meaning 'he caused to be brought/ordered to be brought' (classical causative/active sense): 'he had everyone with something to say brought before them.'
segments.s296-g57: سپهر (subject) + اندر آن جنگ (prepositional time/place phrase) + نظاره بود (predicate) = 'the sky was observing that battle.' اندر = 'in/within'; نظاره بود is a light verbal construction meaning 'was observing/looking on.' Word order is slightly poetic: topic (سپهر) then location then verbal predicate.
segments.s296-g58: ستاره شمر is a possessive juxtaposition meaning 'the star of Shamr' (noun + name). سخت here is an adverbial intensifier 'very/terribly,' modifying the adjective/nominal بیچاره 'poor wretch / pitiable one.' بود is the past copula 'was.' The phrase = 'Shamr's star was terribly pitiable.'
segments.s296-g59: به‌روز چهارم forms a temporal adverbial 'on the fourth day.' چو شد is a temporal clause marker + past verb 'when it became,' and کار تنگ means 'the affair/situation became tight' — a metaphor for 'the situation became difficult/pressing.' شد is the past intransitive 'became.'
segments.s296-g60: به پیش پدر is a prepositional expression 'before/in the presence of his father.' شد here functions as a past intransitive 'came/appeared' (poetic usage), followed by the subject/predicate nominal دلاور پشنگ 'the valiant Pashang' (epithet + proper name). Poetic word order places شد before the nominal phrase.
segments.s296-g61: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said' — note the lemma senses list lacks the verb sense, so mapped as -1). کای is the archaic/vocative 'O' addressing someone, and کدخدای جهان is the title 'headman/chief of the world' used here as an honorific; the vocative follows the verb phrase in poetic order.
segments.s296-g62: سرافراز is an adjective 'lofty / triumphant,' بر is the preposition 'over/above' governing the coordinated phrase کهتران و مهان ('the low/mean and the great' — a poetic antithesis). The coordination shows his loftiness extends above both classes. Some provided lemma senses do not match the poetic nouns here, so those are marked -1 where appropriate.
segments.s296-g63: به فر تو = 'by/with your far' (فر = royal/divine glory) — a genitive-like phrase 'in your glory.' زیر فلک = 'under the sky/heavens.' شاه نیست = 'there is no king' (شاه as indefinite noun, نیست negates existence). The idiom says: 'Under heaven there is no king equal to you in glory.'
segments.s296-g64: ترا is the clitic object 'to you' (here written split as ت + را in the tokenization), marking the person affected. ماه و خورشید = 'the moon and the sun.' بد خواه is a compound meaning 'ill-wisher' (بد = ill/bad, خواه = one who wishes); نیست negates it. Overall: 'Neither moon nor sun is an ill-wisher toward you.'
segments.s296-g49: از این روی و زان روی is a paired prepositional phrase meaning 'from this side and from that side' (از + demonstrative + روی ‘side’). بر پشت زین is another locative phrase: بر (+ پشت ‘back/rear’) + زین ‘saddle’ = 'upon the back of the saddle'; there is no overt verb here — the action is supplied by the next segment (typical parataxis in poetic Persian). Word order places repeated spatial phrases up front for emphasis.
segments.s296-g50: پیاده functions as an adjective/nominal phrase 'on foot' or 'the infantry' (here 'foot-soldiers'); به پیش is a directional phrase 'toward the front / forward' (به + پیش), اندرون 'into the interior/inner quarters'. همچنین 'likewise/similarly' links this action to the preceding clause. There is an ellipted verb ('came/advanced') implied rather than expressed.
segments.s296-g51: تو گفتی is second-person past ('you said'); جهان is the subject 'the world'; کوه آهن شدست is a predicate with poetic past/perfect شدست (archaic variant of شده‌است) meaning 'has become a mountain of iron.' Word order is Subject–Predicate with the copular/auxiliary in the archaic form شدست.
segments.s296-g52: همان here is emphatic ('that very' / 'the same'); پوشش چرخ is a genitive/construct phrase 'the covering of the sky' (پوشش + چرخ). جوشن شدست is the predicate: جوشن 'armor' + شدست 'has become', i.e. 'has turned into armor.' The clause repeats the verbless nominal structure with the archaic auxiliary.
segments.s296-g53: ستاره شمر is a noun phrase with a genitive: 'the star of Shamr' (proper name as possessor). پیش دو شهریار places that appearance 'in the presence of / before the two kings' (پیش = 'in front of / before' here). There is no verb in this segment; the predicate follows.
segments.s296-g54: پر اندیشه is a predicate phrase 'full of thought / full of concern' (پر = 'full (of)' + اندیشه 'thought'), applying to the rulers mentioned. و زیجها بر کنار links a second result: 'and the zij-tables (زیجها) were set aside' — بر کنار 'at the side / aside' is an idiom meaning 'laid aside.' The verb is again left implicit; the constructions are paratactic.
segments.s296-g55: همی باز is a reiterated adverbial combination ('again and again' / 'still again') giving continuous or repeated action. جستند is a past plural verb 'they searched/searched for' (here transitive: 'sought'); راز سپهر is the object 'the secret(s) of the sky/heavens.'
segments.s296-g56: به صلاب uses به + noun to mean 'with / by firmness' (i.e. 'with resolute firmness'). تا introduces purpose/result ('so that / until'); بر که گردد literally 'onto whom it may turn' — بر + relative که + گردد (گردد here = 'turn / come to') — and به مهر 'toward favor' ('into favor/benefit'). گردد is subjunctive/optative in sense 'turn / be directed,' so the clause expresses searching until they determine to whose side luck/favor will turn.
segments.s296-g137: ببینند is a third-person plural present/subjunctive (optative) form meaning “may they see” or “let them see.” ایرانیان را uses را as the definite/object marker: Iranianān is the direct object. به چشم is an adverbial phrase ‘by/with the eye’ (literally “to the eye”), placed after the object for poetic word order to mean ‘with their own eyes.’
segments.s296-g138: ز دل is a poetic prepositional phrase ‘from the heart.’ کم شود is a passive/intransitive verbal phrase ‘becomes less’ (شود = becomes), here in the subjunctive/optative mood linked to the earlier ببینند. سوگ is the subject (mourning) and با درد و خشم specifies the manner/agents — ‘with grief and anger.’
segments.s296-g139: هم ... نیز is emphatic duplication meaning ‘also / too.’ ایرانیان is the (explicit) subject; چندین سپاه is a quantified noun phrase ‘several / many hosts (armies)’ that will be the object of the following verb. Word order is poetic: subject + quantifier phrase before the verb.
segments.s296-g140: ببینند repeats the optative/subjunctive ‘may they see/witness.’ آیین تخت و کلاه is a compound noun phrase: آیین ‘rite/ceremony’ governing تخت و کلاه ‘throne and hat(crown),’ i.e. the royal ceremonial trappings; this whole phrase is the object of ببینند.
segments.s296-g141: دو لشکر is the noun phrase ‘two armies.’ بر here carries the sense ‘against / upon’ (classical usage with لشکر), and این‌گونه ‘in this way / such’ qualifies them; پر + [noun] is a classical adjective pattern meaning ‘full of …’ so پر درد و خشم = ‘full of pain/grief and anger.’
segments.s296-g142: ستاره is the grammatical subject ‘a star’ (here: a celestial/star or fate-lumen). به ما دارد is literal ‘has (an eye) on us’ (به ما = upon us; دارد = has/keeps). از چرخ چشم places the source/location: ‘an eye from the charkh (heavens/sky),’ i.e. ‘it keeps an eye on us from the heavens.’ Word order is poetic but the clause means the star watches us.
segments.s296-g143: به انبوه جستن is an infinitival/gerundial phrase ‘to rush/seek by the multitude’ (i.e. to attack en masse). نه نیکوست is the negative predicative ‘is not good/proper.’ جنگ at the end names the domain ‘in war’ (topic-comment order common in poetry).
segments.s296-g144: شکستی is ‘a defeat.’ بود is the copula/auxiliary ‘was/is’ and باد here is the classical optative particle (not ‘wind’): together they form an optative/jussive sense ‘may there be / would there be.’ ماند ‘remain / be left’ takes به چنگ ‘in the grasp’ (چنگ = claw/hand, used figuratively as ‘grip’). The clause expresses a wish about the outcome of defeat.
segments.s296-g153: مرا آرزو جنگ کیخسروست functions as a nominal predicate: 'مرا آرزو' = 'my desire' (classical Persian uses مرا + noun to express 'I desire / my wish is…'). جنگ کیخسروست treats کیخسرو as possessor: کیخسرو + -ست (archaic third-person copula) yields 'is Kay Khosrow's' or 'belongs to Kay Khosrow.' Word order is compact and poetic, with the copula fused to the name.
segments.s296-g154: که introduces a clause giving the reason or explanation ('for/because'); او is the personal pronoun 'he'; در جهان شهریار نوست is a predicative clause with locative در جهان 'in the world' + شهریار 'sovereign/king' followed by نوست (نو + -ست), an archaic adjectival/copular form meaning 'is new.' The adjective follows the noun, as is normal.
segments.s296-g155: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). جوید is the classical 3rd-person present/subjunctive of جستن 'to seek' and is followed by the pronoun او ('he'), a typical classical order where verb can precede subject. بی‌گمان is an inserted adverb 'undoubtedly' and جنگ من = 'my battle' (جنگ followed by the possessor من).
segments.s296-g156: رهایی نیابد is a negated present form: ن‑ + یابد (from یابیدن) meaning 'does not / will not obtain/find release'; in classical Persian the present can have a future force. ز + چنگال من is a prepositional phrase 'from my talon/grasp.' The whole clause says he will not obtain freedom from my clutches.
segments.s296-g157: دل و پشت ایشان شکسته شود is a passive/resultative construction: دل و پشت (heart and back) + ایشان ('their') and شکسته شود ('shall be broken'). شود is the classical auxiliary/copiular form used to make a passive/resultative sense ('shall be broken'). Word order places the affected body-parts before the auxiliary.
segments.s296-g158: بر آن انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'upon/over that assembly/council'; کار بسته شود is another passive/resultative clause: کار 'the affair' + بسته شود 'be closed/settled.' بسته is adjectival/past participle 'closed' and شود gives the sense 'will be made/shall be (so)'. Together: 'the matter will be settled at that council.'
segments.s296-g159: و‌گر is a conditional particle 'and if / but if.' دیگری 'another' is the potential opponent; پیشم 'before me / in my presence' is a clitic-like locative (پیش + ـم for 'my/at me') placed after the noun/verb as in classical usage. آید is 'he/it comes' and به جنگ ('to battle') indicates purpose — 'comes to fight.'
segments.s296-g160: به خاک اندر آرم سرش بی‌درنگ is a compact, poetic action clause: به خاک اندر = 'into the earth / to the ground' (به + خاک + اندر 'into'), آرم is a first-person verb form ('I put / I place' in context; here the listed glosses did not include this specific transitive sense so it's treated as irregular/poetic), سرش 'his head' is the object (سر + -ش genitive), and بی‌درنگ 'immediately' is an adverbial modifier. The overall order (prepositional phrase — verb — object — adverb) is compressed for poetic effect.
segments.s296-g81: هر آن کس که ... کند is a relative clause introduced by هر (lit. “every/any”) + آن کس (’that person’ = ’whoever’). که introduces the subordinate clause. نیکی فرامش کند uses فرامش (a noun ‘forgetfulness/neglect’) with کند (do/make) to form an older, poetic periphrasis meaning ‘forgets (a) goodness/benefaction’; را would normally mark the object but is absent here. The overall phrase means ‘whoever forgets kindness’.
segments.s296-g82: همی ... کند: همی is a classical particle giving continuous or emphatic aspect (’continually/indeed’) before the verb کند (do/make). رای جان سیاوش is a noun phrase where رای (‘decision, vote, counsel’) takes جان (‘life/soul’) in a genitive-like relation: رایِ جان = ‘a decision concerning (someone’s) life’. سیاوش names whose life is meant. Thus همی رای جان سیاوش کند means ‘(he) will decide/bring about Siavash’s fate.’
segments.s296-g83: بپروردی is an optative/jussive form (be‑ + پروردی) used to express a wish or command ‘may you rear/raise’. این شوم ناپاک را is the definite direct object (را marks it): این (=’this’) modifies شوم (‘ill‑omened/wretched’) and ناپاک (‘impure’), so the phrase means ‘this wretched, impure one’.
segments.s296-g84: پدروار is an adverbial/ adjectival modifier meaning ‘in a fatherly way’. نسپردیش is a negative verb form here meaning ‘do not entrust/hand over (him)’, where the clitic -یش refers back to the preceding demonstrative/inanimate object. خاک را is the object ‘the earth/the dust’ (marked by را) — idiomatically ‘commit him to the earth’ = ‘bury him’. So the clause commands not to hand him over to the earth as a father would.
segments.s296-g85: همی داشتی is a classical continuous/past sense: همی + داشتی indicates you continued to hold/have (you kept). تا introduces a temporal/result clause ‘until’ and is followed by بر آورد, a compound verb (بر‑ + آورد ‘bring/cause’), here meaning ‘brought about’ or ‘came to produce’. پر after آورد is an adjective ‘full (of)’, so تا بر آورد پر = ‘until it became full’/‘until it brought fullness’.
segments.s296-g86: شد is the past/perfective ‘became’. از مهر شاه uses از in the ablative/instrumental sense ‘from/because of’ + مهر ‘affection, love’ and شاه ‘the king’ — ‘because of the king’s love’. از در تاج زر = ‘from/of the door/threshold of the golden crown’, where در here is the noun ‘door/gate’ and تاج زر is a genitive sequence ‘golden crown’. The clause describes the result: it became filled owing to the king’s affection, affecting the crown’s threshold imagery.
segments.s296-g87: ز (poetic form of از) marks origin ‘from’ and takes توران (Turan) as its source. چو introduces a simile ‘like/as’; مرغی is a descriptive noun ‘a bird’ (here used adverbially ‘like a bird’). به ایران پرید is the motion clause: به (to/into) + ایران (Iran) + پرید (past of پریدن ‘to fly/leave’), so ‘he flew from Turan into Iran like a bird.’
segments.s296-g88: تو گفتی که is the report clause ‘you said that…’. (Note: in classical usage گفتی here functions as ‘you said’.) که introduces the content clause. هرگز is an adverb ‘never’, نیا را is the object marked by را (naming the person/item not seen), and ندید is the negative past ‘did not see’. Together: ‘you said that you never saw Niyā.’
segments.s296-g121: همان + پیل is a determiner + noun: 'that/the same elephant.' بد روز جنگ is a compact poetic phrase meaning 'ill-fated (bad-day) of battle' (an attributive phrase modifying the elephant). او is a resumptive pronoun ('he') and به زور is an adverbial phrase ('with strength/ by force'); the finite verb is omitted (typical in epic poetry), so this chunk functions as a nominal/predicative description: 'that elephant, [he] by force...'.
segments.s296-g122: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; دریا دل literally 'sea heart' is a poetic inversion for 'a heart like the sea.' The conjunction و links a parallel simile رخ چو تابنده هور ('a face like the shining splendor'). No verb is expressed; these are predicative similes completing the description begun in the previous segment.
segments.s296-g123: برادرش is 'his brother' (برادر + possessive suffix ش). هومان is the proper name in apposition. پلنگ is an epithet 'the Panther' applied to him. نبرد here stands as the action/predicate in a compressed, classical style (the noun 'battle' functions to mean 'fought' or 'took part in battle'), so the segment reads 'his brother Hūmān, the Panther, fought.'
segments.s296-g124: چو introduces a comparison 'like/as.' لهاک جنگی is a binomial epithet: لهاک (here treated as an epithet) modified by جنگی 'warlike' — together forming a comparison to a famed warrior. و links the name فرشیدورد as another comparison partner. The clause is elliptical (no explicit verb) and continues the idea 'he fought like ... and ...'.
segments.s296-g125: ز ترکان is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the Turks.' سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders' and کین is an attributive noun/adjective 'vengeance/vengeful' modifying them. صدهزار is a numeral 'one hundred thousand' quantifying the horsemen. The whole phrase names the enemy force.
segments.s296-g126: همه is the universal 'all/everyone' as subject; نامجوی is an adjectival/noun compound 'name-seeking' (i.e. seekers of fame) describing those horsemen. از در کارزار is a locative/ablative phrase 'on/in the field of battle' (از + در used poetically). Together: 'all seekers of renown in the battlefield.'
segments.s296-g127: برفتند is the simple past, 3rd-person plural 'they went/they departed.' از ایدر 'from here' gives the point of departure. پر از جنگ و جوش is a predicate/adverbial cluster 'full of war and ardour' (پر 'full of' followed by از + nouns); it describes their state as they left.
segments.s296-g128: من ایدر 'I, here' marks the speaker; نوان is an adjectival predicate 'youthful' (I, young). با غم و با خروش are parallel prepositional phrases 'with grief and with tumult/uprising,' describing the speaker's emotional state. Again the verb is omitted; the clause is descriptive.
segments.s296-g97: The correlative negation 'نه ... نه ...' begins here: 'نه دینار خواهد' = 'he wants neither dinar'. The finite verb خواهد (third-person singular present) follows the first negated item and governs the whole series; تخت و کلاه is a coordinated pair ('throne and crown/hat') that is part of the negated list.
segments.s296-g98: Continuation of the same negative list without repeating the verb: the second 'نه' (neither) prefaces another coordinated series. The verb خواهد is understood from the previous segment (ellipsis of the verb). The particles 'و' simply coordinate the nouns (horse, sword, treasure, army).
segments.s296-g99: ز خویشان = 'from/among (his) kin' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from/among'). جز از جان = 'except (from) life' (جز ... از is the idiom 'except for ...'). نخواهد is third-person singular 'wants/asks' and together the clause means 'he wants nothing from his relatives except life.' همی is an archaic continuous/emphatic particle (adds a habitual or emphatic flavor).
segments.s296-g100: سخن را is the (definite) object marked by را ('the speech/word'). از این در is an adverbial phrase 'in this matter' or 'concerning this' (از here = 'about/concerning'); نکاهد همی is a poetic negative/factive form meaning 'he will not lessen' or 'he will not take away' (ن- prefix + کاهد), with همی again adding an archaic continuous/emphatic sense. The whole clause: 'he will not diminish his speech concerning this.'
segments.s296-g101: پدر شاه is a classical/genitive juxtaposition 'the father of the king' (no ezāfe shown in older/poetic style). و فرزانه‌تر adds a coordinated descriptive phrase 'and the more-wise' (comparative adjective). پادشاست is the predicate 'is (a) king/sovereign' in third-person singular, so the clause says roughly 'the king's father, and the wiser (one), is the sovereign.'
segments.s296-g102: بدین is the prepositional phrase 'by/with this' (ب = 'by/with' + this). راست‌گفتار is a compound noun 'truthful speech' and من is the speaker's pronoun 'my' (postposed possession is idiomatic here: 'my truthful speech'). بر + گواست functions as 'is witness to / stands witness upon' in a poetic construction: literally 'I am witness upon (it),' so the clause means 'by this truthful speech I am witness.'
segments.s296-g103: This is an inverted/existential-negative construction: از ایرانیان نیست چندین سخن literally 'of Iranians there are not many words' — i.e. 'Iranians are not given to many words' or 'it is not the custom of Iranians to speak at length.' از here marks the class/group ('of/among Iranians'), نیست is the negative existential 'is not,' and چندین سخن is the phrase 'many a word / many words.'
segments.s296-g104: سپه را is the direct object with the definite marker را ('the army'). چنین is an adverb 'thus/so.' دل‌شکسته is a participial/adjectival phrase 'broken‑hearted' describing the resulting state. مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' (addressing someone): 'Do not make the army thus broken‑hearted.'
segments.s296-g73: A nominal phrase: یکی ('a certain') + باد ناخوش ('an unpleasant wind') followed by the prepositional phrase ز رویِ هوا ('from the face of the air/sky'). The order is determiner–noun–modifier; رویِ هوا is an idiomatic poetic locution meaning the surface/face of the air (literally) and thus an unexpected gust or capricious misfortune.
segments.s296-g74: بر او برگذشتی is a 2nd-person past verb phrase: بر او ('upon/over him') + برگذشتی ('you passed by/over'). نبودی روا is a classical judgemental clause: نبودی (past copula/negation 'was not') + روا ('fitting, permissible'). The construction inverts modern expected order: verb phrase + copula + predicate, yielding 'it was not fitting that you passed him by.'
segments.s296-g75: از او سیر گشتی is an intransitive perfective phrase: از او ('of/from him') + سیر گشتی ('you became sated/tired of'). چو کردی درست is a temporal clause: چو ('when') + کردی (2nd-person past 'you did') + درست ('rightly/properly'). Together: 'You became tired of him when you did what was right.'
segments.s296-g76: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'): او ... جست is a 3rd-person past ('he sought/seized'). The list تاج و تخت و سپاه تو places the possessed pronoun تو after سپاه (classical ordering): 'your crown, throne, and army.' جست here means 'sought/aimed at' rather than physical leaping.
segments.s296-g77: گر is the conditional 'if'. او را نکشتی (here intended as a negative past: 'you had not killed him') places the object marker را after او. جهاندار شاه is a vocative/title appended after the clause ('O world-ruling king'). The clause reads as a classical conditional with the address following the protasis.
segments.s296-g78: بدو ('to him') is the indirect-object prepositional phrase; باز is an adverb 'again' and گشتی is a past 2nd-person verb ('you turned/returned' or here 'you would have restored'). نگین و کلاه are the direct objects 'the signet/gem and the crown/hat.' In a counterfactual conditional the past tense here expresses the expected consequence ('you would have returned to him the signet and crown').
segments.s296-g79: کنون اینک are two synonymous temporal adverbs ('now' and 'behold/now') used poetically for emphasis. آمد is simple past 'he came'; به پیشت ('into your presence/before you') and به جنگ ('to/for battle') mark purpose/location: 'he has come before you for battle.'
segments.s296-g80: نباید is an impersonal modal negative ('one must not' / 'you should not'); به گیتی ('in the world' / 'in this life') is an adverbial phrase that intensifies the admonition; فراوان درنگ ('much delay') is the quantified noun phrase. The whole clause means roughly 'you must not delay much (in this world),' i.e. do not tarry.

sentences.s296-s85: Five sons are alive at your side; we will not stand idle until you decide on battle.
sentences.s296-s86: Neither the army nor the god-worshipper will approve, for you make war on him beforehand.
sentences.s296-s17: When Khosrow saw that army of the ancestors, the king's heart became full of the elixir.
sentences.s296-s18: He himself, Rostam, Tous, Goudarz, and Giv — among the host there were many famous ney (reed‑flute) players.
sentences.s296-s19: He kept circling around that battlefield; the desert kept watch all around, off the road and on it.
sentences.s296-s20: For the host was vast; when one tried to count it, one encountered nothing but those very ragged elephants and crowds of men.
sentences.s296-s81: He said to him, "O rash man of heedless deeds, what shahanshah would seek battle with you?"
sentences.s296-s82: If you seek his battle, I too am (ready); I will cast his body and name beneath my feet.
sentences.s296-s83: If he comes with me to the battlefield, both armies will be relieved from war.
sentences.s296-s84: The lover said to him, 'O worldly‑wise man, you have tasted much of the world — both heat and cold.'
sentences.s296-s5: The grandees of Iran selected an army from the war-bringers, as was fitting.
sentences.s296-s6: He had tasted much of the world's tumult and bitterness; he went to Balkh to help Nowzar.
sentences.s296-s7: With that tear he commanded that the army, the elephants, and the treasure (in dirhams) be taken toward the land.
sentences.s296-s8: Do this so that thereafter the army will not enter within, and the counsel of Iran's lions be ruined.
sentences.s296-s45: Beware of kindness, for see what the elders did to that faithless, reproachable man.
sentences.s296-s46: He forgot all the elders' affection, and filled head and heart with hatred and boiling anger.
sentences.s296-s47: He remained silent; when he came to strike with his fist, he thus killed that kind hero.
sentences.s296-s48: Now he came from Iran with an army to fight, and appeared before Niyâ Tizchang.
sentences.s296-s33: A mountain of iron would become like a sea of water if it heard the name of Afrasiab.
sentences.s296-s34: Neither will the earth bear your host, nor the radiant sun your crown.
sentences.s296-s35: No one of the kings will come before you except this fatherless, ill-born wretch — your own worthless jewel.
sentences.s296-s36: You kept Siavash as a son; toward him you had a father's anguish and affection.
sentences.s296-s13: On the left lay Dehestān and on the right (was) water; sand was between them, and Afrasiāb stood in front.
sentences.s296-s14: When the sun rose from the tower, it adorned the entire surface of the earth.
sentences.s296-s15: The Turks' commander saw the army; he struck the iron trumpet and formed the ranks.
sentences.s296-s16: The world was filled with the sound of trumpets, and the whole army lifted (their) iron helmets.
sentences.s296-s65: Because of him who was slain on this plain, the ground beneath him became soaked like clay.
sentences.s296-s66: All the borders of Turan are broken‑hearted; from solicitude they ever rend the heart.
sentences.s296-s67: They see nothing in sleep but the death of the elders; no one will utter the name Afrasiab.
sentences.s296-s68: Let us be so that our renowned men are guests, and that our horsemen are from the host.
sentences.s296-s73: We will drive out their scattered fighters and fill the plains with blood.
sentences.s296-s74: He answered thus: 'O king, if you seek a battle of this sort...'
sentences.s296-s75: I am the foremost of the fighting host, who fling my steed upon lion and upon elephant.
sentences.s296-s76: I know no one who, on the day of battle, would hurl (a missile) at my horse from afar.
sentences.s296-s9: From that time on he marshaled all the warriors, struck the iron drum, and drove the host forward.
sentences.s296-s10: He proceeded with counsel, intelligence, and deliberation, for swiftness brings remorse in battle.
sentences.s296-s11: When the commander reached the plain, he saw the charge, the music, and the host.
sentences.s296-s12: The army’s passage was toward Khwarazm; the whole plain and field were full of the signs of battle.
sentences.s296-s21: He dug on one side around the army; he scattered the vanguard to every side.
sentences.s296-s22: Night fell; he poured water into the ditch he had dug on that side which faced Afrasiab.
sentences.s296-s23: Thus the two armies remained so for two days and two nights; from them not one moved a lip.
sentences.s296-s24: You said that the surface of the earth is iron; spears fill the air, and armor is there as well.
sentences.s296-s57: When Afrasiab heard these words he said to him, "Hurry, but do not be violent."
sentences.s296-s58: Everything you said was entirely true; nevertheless, one should not hear anything except the truth.
sentences.s296-s59: But you know that the elders entrusted every path of goodness to the world.
sentences.s296-s60: There was no crookedness or deficiency in his heart; you would find nothing except goodness and truth.
sentences.s296-s53: What need have they for Shamr's star? Men of skill seek (it) with the sword.
sentences.s296-s54: The horsemen who are on the right flank with me — all in battle are of one heart and one body.
sentences.s296-s55: If the king commands me, I will not leave even one pious man among them.
sentences.s296-s56: I will fasten their heads and rags to arrows; I will not trouble myself about stump or pool.
sentences.s296-s1: When the sovereign of the world became aware through the warning words of the vigilant informants.
sentences.s296-s2: Because of the Turks and by Afrasiab's action — who brought his encampment to this side of the water.
sentences.s296-s3: An army crossed the Jeyhun to this side, so that sand and stone vanished from the world.
sentences.s296-s4: When Khosrow heard (this), he summoned the heroes and had everyone who had anything to say brought before them.
sentences.s296-s29: The heavens watched that battle; the star of Shamr was sorely distressed.
sentences.s296-s30: On the fourth day, when the affair grew tight, the brave Pashang came before his father.
sentences.s296-s31: He said to him, 'O headman of the world, lofty above the low and the great.'
sentences.s296-s32: Beneath the heavens there is no king like you in royal glory; neither the moon nor the sun is an ill-wisher of you.
sentences.s296-s25: From this side and from that side, (men) on the saddle-backs — and likewise the foot-soldiers advanced forward into the interior.
sentences.s296-s26: You said the world has become a mountain of iron; that very covering of the sky has become armor.
sentences.s296-s27: Shamr's star, before the two kings, filled them with thought, and the zij-tables were put aside.
sentences.s296-s28: Again they sought the secrets of the heavens with firmness, until they could tell to whom it would turn in favor.
sentences.s296-s69: May they behold the Iranians with their eyes; may the mourning in the heart be lessened by grief and wrath.
sentences.s296-s70: May the Iranians also behold many hosts, and witness the ceremonial pomp of throne and crown.
sentences.s296-s71: Two armies, in this manner full of grief and rage — a star keeps its eye upon us from the heavens.
sentences.s296-s72: To rush on in great numbers is not proper in war; may a defeat remain in the grip.
sentences.s296-s77: My wish is the battle of Kay Khosrow, for he is a new sovereign in the world.
sentences.s296-s78: If he seeks battle with me, surely he will not find release from my grasp.
sentences.s296-s79: Their hearts and backs will be broken; upon that assembly the affair will be brought to a close.
sentences.s296-s80: And if another comes before me to fight, I will immediately lay his head in the dust.
sentences.s296-s41: Whoever forgets goodness will determine Siavash's fate.
sentences.s296-s42: May you rear this wretched, unclean one; do not commit him to the earth as a father would.
sentences.s296-s43: You kept it on until it brought fullness; from the king’s affection the threshold of the golden crown became filled.
sentences.s296-s44: From Turan he flew into Iran like a bird; you said that you never saw Niyā.
sentences.s296-s61: That same ill-fated war-elephant — he was mighty, his heart like the sea and his face like radiant splendor.
sentences.s296-s62: His brother Hūmān, the Panther, fought like the warlike Lahāk and Farshidvard.
sentences.s296-s63: From the Turks came a hundred thousand vengeful horsemen, all seekers of renown on the battlefield.
sentences.s296-s64: They departed from here, full of war and ardour; I here, youthful, with grief and with tumult.
sentences.s296-s49: He desires neither dinars nor throne and crown; neither horse nor sword, nor treasure or an army.
sentences.s296-s50: From his kin he will ask for nothing but life; he will not lessen his words about this.
sentences.s296-s51: The king's father — the wiser (one) — is the sovereign; by this truthful speech I am witness.
sentences.s296-s52: It is not the way of Iranians to utter many words; do not make the army thus broken‑hearted.
sentences.s296-s37: A certain unpleasant wind from the face of the air fell upon him; it was not fitting that you passed him by.
sentences.s296-s38: You grew weary of him when you acted rightly, for he sought your crown, throne, and army.
sentences.s296-s39: Had you not killed him, O sovereign king, you would have returned to him the signet and crown.
sentences.s296-s40: Now — behold — he has come before you to battle; you must not delay much in the world.
